Rather than introducing a complex architecture, we use a deliberately simple dual-head model with a shared encoder and two task-specific heads as a probe of hierarchical supervision. We compare single-OA, single-KL, and dual-head training across multiple 3D backbones under the same test protocol. Beyond standard classification metrics, we perform paired statistical comparisons, analyze latent severity-axis geometry, and examine saliency overlap with cartilage regions. The results show that dual-head supervision produces backbone-dependent gains, with clear improvements in KL-related metrics for selected backbones. More importantly, the gains are accompanied by a more ordered coarse-to-fine latent organization and, for responsive backbones, stronger anatomical alignment of saliency with cartilage. These findings suggest that even simple hierarchical dual-head supervision can reshape disease representations under noisy coarse/fine labels, providing a useful inductive bias for OA diagnosis and severity grading.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether hierarchical supervision (coarse OA presence + fine-grained KL grading) using a simple dual-head model can improve knee osteoarthritis assessment over single-task approaches, finding backbone-dependent gains in KL metrics, more ordered latent representations, and better anatomical alignment.
AI.motivation: Knee OA assessment has a natural label hierarchy (binary OA + KL severity grades) that is often ignored; existing methods treat these as separate problems, missing potential representation-level supervisory benefits.
AI.method: Simple dual-head model with shared encoder and two task-specific heads; compared single-OA, single-KL, and dual-head training across multiple 3D backbones under same protocol with paired stats, latent geometry analysis, and saliency mapping.
AI.result: Dual-head supervision yields backbone-dependent gains, improving KL metrics for selected backbones, with more ordered coarse-to-fine latent organization and stronger cartilage saliency alignment.
AI.conclusion: Even simple hierarchical dual-head supervision can reshape disease representations under noisy coarse/fine labels, providing a useful inductive bias for OA diagnosis and severity grading.

title: Unpaired Image Deraining Using Reward-Guided Self-Reinforcement Strategy
summary: Unsupervised deraining has attracted attention for its ability to learn the real-world distribution of rain without paired supervision. However, the lack of strong constraints makes it difficult for the network to converge, especially with the complex diversity of rain degradation. A key motivation is that high-quality deraining results occasionally emerge during training, which can be leveraged to guide the optimization process. To overcome these challenges, we introduce RGSUD (Reward-Guided Self-Reinforcement Unsupervised Image Deraining), comprising two key stages: reward recycling and self-reinforcement (SR) training. For the former stage, we propose an Image Quality Assessment (IQA)-based dynamic reward recycling mechanism that selects optimal derained outputs during training and continuously collects high-quality deraining images. In latter stage, we incorporate these rewards into the model's optimization process, constraining the optimization space and improving alignment between derained outputs and clean images. By leveraging IQA-based self-reinforced loss and dynamically updated rewards, we enhance the quality of synthesized pseudo-paired data and stabilize the optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves SOTA performance across multiple datasets, including paired synthetic, paired real, and unpaired real images, outperforming existing unsupervised deraining approaches in both subjective and objective IQA metrics. Additionally, we show that the self-reinforcement strategy is adaptable to other unsupervised deraining methods and our deraining framework demonstrates strong generalization across existing supervised deraining networks.

AI.tldr: RGSUD introduces a reward-guided self-reinforcement mechanism for unsupervised image deraining, using dynamic IQA-based reward recycling and self-reinforcement training to improve optimization and achieve SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised deraining suffers from weak constraints due to lack of paired supervision, making network convergence difficult amid complex rain diversity. High-quality deraining results occasionally emerge during training but are not effectively leveraged.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) Reward recycling with IQA-based dynamic reward mechanism to select and collect optimal derained outputs; (2) Self-reinforcement training incorporating these rewards into optimization via IQA-based self-reinforced loss to constrain the optimization space and improve deraining quality.
AI.result: SOTA performance across paired synthetic, paired real, and unpaired real datasets, outperforming existing unsupervised methods in both subjective and objective IQA metrics.
AI.conclusion: The self-reinforcement strategy is adaptable to other unsupervised deraining methods, and the framework demonstrates strong generalization across existing supervised deraining networks.

title: Exploring the Limits of End-to-End Feature-Affinity Propagation for Single-Point Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection
summary:
Single-point supervised infrared small target detection (IRSTD) drastically reduces dense annotation costs. Current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods achieve high precision by recovering mask supervision through explicit, offline pseudo-label construction, such as multi-stage active learning and physics-driven mask generation. In this paper, we study a minimalist alternative: generating point-to-mask supervision online through in-batch, point-anchored feature-affinity propagation. We instantiate this paradigm as GSACP, an end-to-end testbed that directly supervises the detector using hard-margin feature affinity gated by local image priors, entirely eliminating external label-evolution loops.
  This compact design, however, exposes an optimization bottleneck. Because the affinity target is generated from the same feature representation being optimized, training forms a self-referential loop. We theoretically formalize this as \emph{Self-Referential Propagation Drift}, a representation-supervision entanglement that can sharpen true boundaries or distort the feature space to satisfy its own targets. To systematically isolate these failure modes, we apply a protocolized single-variable ablation procedure spanning local EMA teacher decoupling, hard-background contrastive separation, and adaptive support geometry.
  On the SIRST3 dataset, GSACP-Final establishes a new ultra-low false-alarm operating regime, achieving a highly competitive $0.6674$ mIoU while demonstrating a $38\% relative reduction in false-positive artifacts ($\mathrm{Fa}$) compared with PAL. By systematically deconstructing the end-to-end paradigm, we map its performance boundaries and show that in-batch feature propagation provides a compact alternative for deployment scenarios where false-alarm suppression is paramount.

AI.tldr: GSACP proposes an end-to-end single-point supervised IRSTD method that uses in-batch point-anchored feature-affinity propagation to generate pseudo-masks online, eliminating external label-evolution loops. It identifies and mitigates Self-Referential Propagation Drift through decoupling strategies, achieving competitive mIoU with 38% fewer false positives.
AI.motivation: To drastically reduce annotation costs for IRSTD while maintaining high performance, by replacing explicit offline pseudo-label construction (active learning, physics-driven mask generation) with a minimalist end-to-end online alternative.
AI.method: GSACP generates point-to-mask supervision online via hard-margin feature affinity propagation gated by local image priors, using in-batch point-anchored feature-affinity. A protocolized single-variable ablation (local EMA teacher decoupling, hard-background contrastive separation, adaptive support geometry) systematically addresses the Self-Referential Propagation Drift issue.
AI.result: On SIRST3, GSACP-Final achieves 0.6674 mIoU with a 38% relative reduction in false-positive artifacts (Fa) compared to PAL, establishing a new ultra-low false-alarm operating regime.
AI.conclusion: In-batch feature propagation provides a compact, end-to-end alternative for deployment scenarios where false-alarm suppression is paramount; the study systematically maps the performance boundaries of the end-to-end paradigm.

title: Quantum Gradient-Based Approach for Edge and Corner Detection Using Sobel Kernels
summary: Edge detection refers to identifying points in a digital image where intensity changes sharply, indicating object boundaries or structural features. Corners are locations where gray-level intensity changes abruptly in multiple directions and are widely used in feature extraction, object tracking, and 3D modeling. In this study, we present a quantum implementation of Sobel-based edge detection and Harris-style corner detection. Two quantum image encoding methods - Flexible Representation of Quantum Images (FRQI) and Quantum Probability Image Encoding (QPIE) - are used to encode the input data and are comparatively analyzed. The proposed approach introduces a quantum gradient computation scheme based on lag-2 differences, enabling the evaluation of gradient-like features in superposition. To improve detection quality and reduce false positives, a classical post-processing step is applied to candidate corner points identified by the quantum circuit. Results show that the proposed quantum circuits produce outputs consistent with classical Sobel and Harris operators. Furthermore, the QPIE-based configuration yields more stable and coherent results than FRQI, especially under limited measurement shots. While gradient computation can be performed efficiently at the circuit level, the overall cost remains dominated by state preparation, measurement, and classical post-processing. All experiments are conducted under noiseless simulation, and performance on NISQ hardware may be affected by noise and measurement limitations. Therefore, this work demonstrates a functional and scalable quantum realization of classical edge and corner detection methods rather than an end-to-end speedup.

AI.tldr: This paper presents quantum implementations of Sobel edge detection and Harris corner detection using FRQI and QPIE image encodings, with a quantum gradient computation scheme based on lag-2 differences. QPIE outperforms FRQI under limited measurement shots, though overall cost remains dominated by state preparation and post-processing.
AI.motivation: To explore quantum computing's potential for classical image processing tasks (edge and corner detection) by implementing quantum versions of Sobel and Harris operators, and to compare two quantum image encoding methods (FRQI vs. QPIE).
AI.method: Two quantum image encodings (FRQI and QPIE) are used. A lag-2 difference-based quantum gradient computation scheme enables superposition-based gradient evaluation. Classical post-processing reduces false positives in corner detection.
AI.result: Quantum circuits produce outputs consistent with classical Sobel/Harris operators. QPIE yields more stable results than FRQI under limited measurement shots. Gradient computation is efficient at circuit level, but total cost is dominated by state preparation, measurement, and classical post-processing.
AI.conclusion: A functional and scalable quantum realization of classical edge/corner detection is demonstrated, though it is not an end-to-end speedup. Performance on NISQ hardware may be affected by noise and measurement limitations.

title: Modeling Subjective Urban Perception with Human Gaze
summary: Urban perception describes how people subjectively evaluate urban environments, shaping how cities are experienced and understood. Existing computational approaches primarily model urban perception directly from street view images, but largely ignore the human perceptual process through which such judgments are formed. In this paper, we introduce Place Pulse-Gaze, an urban perception dataset that augments street view images with synchronized eye-tracking recordings and individual perception labels. Based on this dataset, we propose a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework to study how gaze behavior contributes to the modeling of subjective urban perception. The framework systematically investigates three complementary settings: gaze-only modeling, gaze fusion with explicit semantic scene representations, and gaze fusion with implicit richer visual representations. Experiments show that gaze alone already carries useful predictive signals for subjective urban perception, and that integrating gaze with scene representations further improves prediction under both semantic and richer visual representations. Overall, our findings highlight the importance of incorporating human perceptual processes into urban scene understanding and open a direction for gaze-guided multimodal urban computing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Place Pulse-Gaze, an urban perception dataset with eye-tracking data, and proposes a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework showing that gaze behavior improves prediction of subjective urban perception.
AI.motivation: Existing computational models for urban perception ignore the human perceptual process (e.g., gaze behavior) and directly predict from street view images.
AI.method: Created Place Pulse-Gaze dataset (street images + eye-tracking + perception labels); proposed Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework with three settings: gaze-only, gaze fusion with semantic scene representations, and gaze fusion with implicit visual representations.
AI.result: Gaze alone carries predictive signals for urban perception; integrating gaze with scene representations (both semantic and visual) improves prediction performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating human perceptual processes (gaze) is important for urban scene understanding, opening a direction for gaze-guided multimodal urban computing.

title: Map2World: Segment Map Conditioned Text to 3D World Generation
summary: 3D world generation is essential for applications such as immersive content creation or autonomous driving simulation. Recent advances in 3D world generation have shown promising results; however, these methods are constrained by grid layouts and suffer from inconsistencies in object scale throughout the entire world. In this work, we introduce a novel framework, Map2World, that first enables 3D world generation conditioned on user-defined segment maps of arbitrary shapes and scales, ensuring global-scale consistency and flexibility across expansive environments. To further enhance the quality, we propose a detail enhancer network that generates fine details of the world. The detail enhancer enables the addition of fine-grained details without compromising overall scene coherence by incorporating global structure information. We design the entire pipeline to leverage strong priors from asset generators, achieving robust generalization across diverse domains, even under limited training data for scene generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches in user-controllability, scale consistency, and content coherence, enabling users to generate 3D worlds under more complex conditions.

AI.tldr: Map2World enables 3D world generation from user-defined segment maps of arbitrary shapes and scales, using a detail enhancer network for fine-grained details while maintaining global consistency, outperforming existing methods in controllability and coherence.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D world generation methods are constrained by grid layouts and suffer from inconsistencies in object scale across the entire world, limiting flexibility for expansive environments.
AI.method: Map2World: (1) conditioned on user-defined segment maps of arbitrary shapes/scales; (2) detail enhancer network that adds fine details using global structure information; (3) leverages strong priors from asset generators for robust generalization.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing approaches in user-controllability, scale consistency, and content coherence, enabling 3D world generation under more complex conditions.
AI.conclusion: Map2World provides flexible, globally consistent 3D world generation with fine-grained details, robust across diverse domains even with limited training data.

title: GMGaze: MoE-Based Context-Aware Gaze Estimation with CLIP and Multiscale Transformer
summary: Gaze estimation methods commonly use facial appearances to predict the direction of a person gaze. However, previous studies show three major challenges with convolutional neural network (CNN)-based, transformer-based, and contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP)-based methods, including late fusion of image features, lack of factor-aware conditioning, and impractical capacity scaling. To address these challenges, we propose Globally-conditioned Multi-scale Gaze estimation (GMGaze), which leverages a multi-scale transformer architecture. Specifically, the model first introduces semantic prototype conditioning, which modulates the CLIP global image embedding using four learned prototype banks (i.e., illumination, background, head pose and appearance) to generate two complementary context-biased global tokens. These tokens, along with the CLIP patch and CNN tokens, are fused at the first layer. This early unified fusion prevents information loss common in late-stage merging. Finally, each token passes through sparse Mixture-of-Experts modules, providing conditional computational capacity without uniformly increasing dense parameters. For cross-domain adaptation, we incorporate an adversarial domain adaptation technique with a feature separation loss that encourages the two global tokens to remain de-correlated. Experiments using four public benchmarks (MPIIFaceGaze, EYEDIAP, Gaze360, and ETH-XGaze) show that GMGaze achieves mean angular errors of 2.49$^\circ$, 3.22$^\circ$, 10.16$^\circ$, and 1.44$^\circ$, respectively, outperforming previous baselines in all within-domain settings. In cross-domain evaluations, it provides state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on two standard transfer routes.

AI.tldr: GMGaze proposes a multi-scale transformer for gaze estimation with early fusion of CLIP global embeddings, semantic prototype conditioning, and sparse Mixture-of-Experts, achieving SOTA results on four benchmarks (2.49°–10.16° mean angular error) and in cross-domain settings.
AI.motivation: Address three challenges in gaze estimation: late fusion of image features causing information loss, lack of factor-aware conditioning, and impractical capacity scaling of dense models.
AI.method: Multi-scale transformer with: (1) semantic prototype conditioning using four prototype banks (illumination, background, head pose, appearance) on CLIP global embeddings; (2) early unified fusion of CLIP patch tokens, CNN tokens, and context-biased global tokens at first layer; (3) sparse Mixture-of-Experts for conditional capacity scaling; (4) adversarial domain adaptation with feature separation loss for cross-domain.
AI.result: MPIIFaceGaze: 2.49°, EYEDIAP: 3.22°, Gaze360: 10.16°, ETH-XGaze: 1.44° mean angular error — outperforming all within-domain baselines. SOTA cross-domain on two transfer routes.
AI.conclusion: Early fusion, prototype-conditioned global tokens, and sparse MoE effectively overcome late fusion, lack of conditioning, and capacity scaling issues, setting new SOTA for gaze estimation in both within-domain and cross-domain settings.

title: Let ViT Speak: Generative Language-Image Pre-training
summary: In this paper, we present \textbf{Gen}erative \textbf{L}anguage-\textbf{I}mage \textbf{P}re-training (GenLIP), a minimalist generative pretraining framework for Vision Transformers (ViTs) designed for multimodal large language models (MLLMs). To better align vision encoders with the autoregressive nature of LLMs, GenLIP trains a ViT to predict language tokens directly from visual tokens using a standard language modeling objective, without contrastive batch construction or an additional text decoder. This design offers three key advantages: (1) \textbf{Simplicity}: a single transformer jointly models visual and textual tokens; (2) \textbf{Scalability}: it scales effectively with both data and model size; and (3) \textbf{Performance}: it achieves competitive or superior results across diverse multimodal benchmarks. Trained on 8B samples from Recap-DataComp-1B, GenLIP matches or surpasses strong baselines despite using substantially less pretraining data. After continued pretraining on multi-resolution images at native aspect ratios, GenLIP further improves on detail-sensitive tasks such as OCR and chart understanding, making it a strong foundation for vision encoders in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: GenLIP is a generative pretraining framework that trains ViTs to predict language tokens from visual tokens using language modeling, achieving strong multimodal performance with simplicity and scalability.
AI.motivation: Existing vision encoders for MLLMs are often trained with contrastive objectives (e.g., CLIP) which may not align well with the autoregressive nature of LLMs. GenLIP aims to bridge this gap by directly predicting language tokens from visual tokens.
AI.method: GenLIP trains a Vision Transformer (ViT) to autoregressively predict language tokens from visual tokens using a standard language modeling objective, without contrastive learning or an additional text decoder.
AI.result: Trained on 8B samples from Recap-DataComp-1B, GenLIP matches or surpasses strong baselines with less data. Continued pretraining on multi-resolution images with native aspect ratios further improves OCR and chart understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: GenLIP offers a simple, scalable, and high-performing alternative for vision encoder pretraining in MLLMs, aligning better with autoregressive LLMs.

title: Posterior Augmented Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching (FM) trains a time-dependent vector field that transports samples from a simple prior to a complex data distribution. However, for high-dimensional images, each training sample supervises only a single trajectory and intermediate point, yielding an extremely sparse and high-variance training signal. This under-constrained supervision can cause flow collapse, where the learned dynamics memorize specific source-target pairings, mapping diverse inputs to overly similar outputs, failing to generalize. We introduce Posterior-Augmented Flow Matching (PAFM), a theoretically grounded generalization of FM that replaces single-target supervision with an expectation over an approximate posterior of valid target completions for a given intermediate state and condition. PAFM factorizes this intractable posterior into (i) the likelihood of the intermediate under a hypothesized endpoint and (ii) the prior probability of that endpoint under the condition, and uses an importance sampling scheme to construct a mixture over multiple candidate targets. We prove that PAFM yields an unbiased estimator of the original FM objective while substantially reducing gradient variance during training by aggregating information from many plausible continuation trajectories per intermediate. Finally, we show that PAFM improves over FM by up to 3.4 FID50K across different model scales (SiT-B/2 and SiT-XL/2), different architectures (SiT and MMDiT), and in both class and text conditioned benchmarks (ImageNet and CC12M), with a negligible increase in the compute overhead. Code: https://github.com/gstoica27/PAFM.git.

AI.tldr: Posterior-Augmented Flow Matching (PAFM) improves standard flow matching by reducing training signal sparsity and variance through multi-target posterior supervision, achieving up to 3.4 FID improvement on image generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard flow matching suffers from sparse, high-variance training signals because each training sample supervises only a single trajectory, leading to flow collapse where the model memorizes specific source-target pairings and fails to generalize.
AI.method: PAFM replaces single-target supervision with an expectation over an approximate posterior of valid target completions, factorized into endpoint likelihood under the intermediate state and prior probability of the endpoint. It uses importance sampling to construct a mixture over multiple candidate targets, yielding an unbiased estimator of the original FM objective with reduced gradient variance.
AI.result: PAFM improves FID50K by up to 3.4 across different model scales (SiT-B/2, SiT-XL/2), architectures (SiT, MMDiT), and conditioning settings (class-conditioned ImageNet, text-conditioned CC12M), with negligible compute overhead.
AI.conclusion: PAFM is a theoretically grounded generalization of flow matching that effectively mitigates flow collapse and improves generation quality by aggregating information from multiple plausible continuation trajectories during training.
